serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of sambal fried rice with Nyonya otah, approximately 380 g, comprising a generous portion of mixed-grain fried rice and one to two pieces of grilled Nyonya fish otah.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice (Nasi Goreng) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sambal fried rice with Nyonya otah exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for nasi goreng (fried rice, Singaporean style) with USDA FoodData Central data for fish otah / fish cake components and sambal chilli paste. The dish description notes enrichment with red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, which modestly increases fibre and protein relative to plain white-rice fried rice. Nutrient values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the combined dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed-grain rice and fried rice analogues — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal fried rice with Nyonya otah. GI estimated at 55 based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed-grain rice (GI ~50–55) and fried rice (GI ~55–65 depending on fat and protein content); (2) the dish's enrichment with red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, which lowers GI relative to plain white rice fried rice; (3) the fat and protein contribution from egg, fish otah, and coconut milk, which further attenuates glycaemic response. GI 55 (low category, ≤55 threshold) is the conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 85.5) / 100 = 47.1, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 22.5 × 3.8 = 85.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1596 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 70–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (GL ~47 per serving) from mixed-grain rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a GI of 55 (low category), the large carbohydrate load per serving (~85 g) produces a high glycaemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (approximately half a plate) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk in otah and cooking oil; elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from coconut milk in the Nyonya otah (~2.1 g per 100 g) and the high sodium content are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose preparations with reduced oil and lower-sodium sambal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Fried Rice with Nyonya Otah is a Singaporean Peranakan-influenced dish combining spicy sambal-seasoned fried rice with a side of house-made Nyonya-style otah (otak-otak). The rice base is notably enriched with red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, giving it a higher fibre and protein content than conventional white-rice fried rice, along with a slightly nutty, chewy texture. The sambal paste — a blend of dried chillies, shallots, garlic, belacan (shrimp paste), and aromatics — is stir-fried with the mixed grains, egg, and seasonings to produce a fragrant, moderately spicy, umami-rich rice dish. The Nyonya otah is a spiced fish paste cake made from blended fish, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, kaffir lime leaves, and chilli, wrapped in banana leaf and grilled or steamed until set. It has a soft, custard-like texture with a rich, aromatic, mildly spicy flavour profile characteristic of Peranakan cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~7.2 g) from egg and fish, moderate fat (~6.8 g) including saturated fat from coconut milk, and carbohydrates (~22.5 g) from the mixed-grain rice base. Dietary fibre is modestly elevated (~1.8 g per 100 g) due to the red rice, brown rice, and quinoa enrichment. Sodium is high (~420 mg per 100 g), primarily from sambal paste and seasoning. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low category), but the glycaemic load per full serving is substantial (~47), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is relevant to clinical nutrition counselling for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GI but high GL per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore, served at casual dining and food court settings.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sambal fried rice with Nyonya otah exists in HPB Foodo or any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB Foodo nasi goreng data with USDA FoodData Central fish cake and sambal component data, adjusted for the stated mixed-grain enrichment. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed-grain rice entries. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be verified if HPB Foodo adds a specific sambal fried rice with otah entry. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central source data used for this composite estimate. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source data for nasi goreng or the fish cake component data used. 4. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Oils, seasonings and condiments', which is incorrect for a mixed rice dish; corrected to 'Mixed'. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to be platform metadata artefacts and are not representative of this dish; cuisine is correctly classified as Singaporean / Peranakan. 6. GI of 55 is the conservative upper-bound estimate for this mixed-grain fried rice dish; actual GI may be lower due to the red rice, brown rice, and quinoa enrichment. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Flavours by Saute); verification against official MUIS certification is recommended. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects trace amounts from cooking oil; this is a plausible estimate and not confirmed zero.
